mixed.question: In 2025, researchers at MIT discovered that which common household substance can efficiently break down certain 'forever chemicals' (PFAS)?
mixed.fact: Simple lye at just 80°C can defluorinate some PFAS — no extreme heat or pressure needed.

mixed.question: Which planet in our solar system has a day (one full rotation) that is longer than its year (one full orbit around the Sun)?
mixed.fact: Venus takes 243 Earth days to rotate once but only 225 Earth days to orbit the Sun.

mixed.question: What did scientists at CERN unexpectedly observe in 2025 about antimatter that challenged prevailing theoretical models?
options: Antihelium remained stable far longer than predicted
mixed.fact: Antihelium-3 nuclei survived milliseconds longer than models predicted, hinting at unknown stabilizing interactions.

mixed.question: Who first demonstrated that stomach ulcers are caused by bacteria, famously by drinking a petri dish of H. pylori?
mixed.fact: Barry Marshall drank the bacteria in 1984, developed ulcers, then cured himself with antibiotics.

mixed.question: True or False: There is a species of jellyfish that is biologically immortal, able to revert to its juvenile form after reaching maturity.
mixed.fact: Turritopsis dohrnii can repeatedly revert to polyp stage, theoretically evading natural death indefinitely.

mixed.question: How many hearts does an octopus have?
mixed.fact: Two branchial hearts pump blood through the gills, while one systemic heart circulates it throughout the body.

mixed.question: Which fruit's seeds contain enough cyanogenic compounds that consuming roughly 200 of them could theoretically be lethal to an adult?
mixed.fact: Cherry pits contain amygdalin, which converts to hydrogen cyanide — but you'd have to crush and eat many.

mixed.question: In which country is it illegal to own just one guinea pig, as it is considered animal cruelty due to their social nature?
mixed.fact: Switzerland's animal welfare laws require guinea pigs to have companionship — a matchmaking service even exists!

mixed.question: In Thailand, which item is it technically illegal to step on, as it bears the image of the King?
mixed.fact: Stepping on Thai money is considered disrespectful to the monarchy and falls under lèse-majesté laws.

mixed.question: In which Australian state was it historically illegal to change a light bulb unless you were a licensed electrician?
mixed.fact: Victoria's old electrical safety regulations technically required a licensed electrician for even simple bulb changes.

science.question: What is the name of the rare weather phenomenon where lightning appears to strike upward from tall structures into thunderclouds?
science.fact: Upward lightning accounts for less than 1% of all lightning but skyscrapers and wind turbines trigger it regularly.

science.question: True or False: Antarctica is technically classified as a desert due to its extremely low precipitation.
science.fact: Antarctica receives less than 200mm of precipitation annually, making it the world's largest desert by area.

science.question: In early 2026, the WMO confirmed that which ocean basin experienced its first recorded Category 5-equivalent tropical cyclone south of the equator in the Atlantic?
science.fact: The South Atlantic was long considered too cold and sheared for major hurricanes, but warming seas changed that.

science.question: Which atmospheric layer sits directly above the troposphere and actually gets warmer with increasing altitude?
science.fact: The stratosphere warms because its ozone layer absorbs UV radiation, creating an inverted temperature profile.

science.question: Which autoimmune disease causes the body to attack the myelin sheath surrounding nerve fibers in the central nervous system?
science.fact: The Epstein-Barr virus, which infects 95% of adults, was identified in 2022 as a likely trigger for MS.

science.question: What counterintuitive condition, first described in the 1800s, causes patients to deny ownership of their own paralyzed limb?
science.fact: Patients with somatoparaphrenia may insist their paralyzed arm belongs to a relative or even a stranger.

science.question: Which 2025 medical breakthrough involved the first successful transplant of a genetically edited pig kidney that functioned for over six months in a living human?
science.fact: The patient received a pig kidney with 69 genetic edits to prevent rejection — a record for xenotransplantation.

science.question: How many fundamental forces of nature are currently recognized by the Standard Model of physics?
science.fact: Gravity is by far the weakest — roughly 10^36 times weaker than electromagnetism between two protons.

science.question: Which physicist first proposed that light travels at a finite speed and estimated it by observing eclipses of Jupiter's moon Io?
science.fact: In 1676, Rømer calculated light speed within about 25% of the true value using only a telescope and timing.

science.question: What surprising physical phenomenon causes a spinning hard-boiled egg placed on a table to spontaneously stand upright?
science.fact: Like a tippe top, friction converts the egg's spinning energy into a torque that flips it to spin on its tip.

history.question: Which American Revolution general defected to the British after plotting to surrender West Point, yet earlier was the hero of Saratoga?
history.fact: Arnold was shot in the same leg twice in battle and nearly lost it before his infamous betrayal.

history.question: How many French soldiers approximately served in North America during the American Revolution?
history.fact: France spent roughly 1.3 billion livres supporting American independence, helping bankrupt the French monarchy.

history.question: What unusual tactic did George Washington use to cross the Delaware River on Christmas night 1776?
options: He disguised boats as British vessels
He used Durham cargo boats designed for iron ore
He built pontoon bridges from church pews
He floated troops on log rafts
history.fact: Durham boats were 40–60 feet long, originally built to haul iron ore and grain downstream.

history.question: In 2025, a previously unknown letter by which Revolutionary War figure was auctioned, revealing secret peace overtures to Britain in 1778?
history.fact: Franklin simultaneously pursued secret diplomacy with Britain while negotiating the French alliance.

history.question: Which ancient Mesopotamian city is considered the world's first true urban center, reaching roughly 40,000 inhabitants by 3000 BCE?
history.fact: Uruk's city walls were legendarily attributed to the hero-king Gilgamesh and stretched over six miles.

history.question: True or False: The ancient Sumerians invented a form of schooling where students practiced writing by copying beer recipes.
history.fact: Sumerians had over 20 beer recipes; students copied hymns to Ninkasi, the goddess of brewing.

history.question: What surprising mathematical concept did Babylonian clay tablets demonstrate knowledge of, over 1,000 years before Pythagoras?
history.fact: Tablet Plimpton 322 (c. 1800 BCE) lists Pythagorean triples with remarkable sophistication and accuracy.

history.question: Which Soviet leader proposed the 'Open Skies' mutual aerial surveillance plan that the U.S. had originally suggested in 1955?
history.fact: Eisenhower first proposed Open Skies in 1955; it was rejected until Gorbachev finally embraced it in 1989.

history.question: During the Cold War, which country secretly hosted both American and Soviet military bases simultaneously?
history.fact: Somalia hosted Soviet bases in the north and then switched to American support after the 1977 Ogaden War.

history.question: What was the code name of the CIA operation that secretly funded abstract expressionist art to counter Soviet cultural influence?
history.fact: The CIA covertly promoted Jackson Pollock and others, believing abstract art symbolized American freedom.

popculture.question: Which superhero was originally going to be named 'Super-Man' with a hyphen and was initially conceived as a villain in 1933?
popculture.fact: Jerry Siegel first wrote Superman as a bald telepathic villain before reimagining him as a hero.

popculture.question: In Marvel's 'Thunderbolts*' (2025), which actor portrayed the team's reluctant leader, Bucky Barnes?
popculture.fact: Sebastian Stan has played Bucky Barnes across more MCU projects than any other Winter Soldier storyline actor.

popculture.question: True or False: In the original comics, Wolverine was first introduced as an enemy of the Hulk, not the X-Men.
options: He debuted in his own solo title
popculture.fact: Wolverine first appeared in The Incredible Hulk #181 (1974) as a Canadian government agent fighting Hulk.

popculture.question: Which comic book publisher created the character Static Shock, who returned in a new 2025 animated series?
popculture.fact: Milestone Media was founded by Black creators in 1993 specifically to increase diversity in comics.

popculture.question: Which 2025 streaming series became Netflix's most-watched non-English show of all time within its first week?
options: All of Us Are Dead Season 2
popculture.fact: Squid Game's global phenomenon made the Korean children's game 'ddakji' a worldwide search trend.

popculture.question: What is the fictional mega-corporation in the Apple TV+ series 'Severance' that performs the controversial severance procedure?
popculture.fact: The Lumon office set was built inside an actual former Bell Labs facility in New Jersey.

popculture.question: How many episodes comprised the first season of HBO's 'The Last of Us,' which broke the channel's streaming records?
popculture.fact: Episode 3, 'Long Long Time,' became HBO's most-tweeted episode of any show in over two years.

popculture.question: The 'Distracted Boyfriend' stock photo meme was taken by a photographer from which country?
popculture.fact: Photographer Antonio Guillem shot the entire series in Girona, Spain, featuring the same three models.

popculture.question: Which 2025 viral moment involved an AI chatbot that millions of users 'befriended' before it was abruptly shut down, sparking global outcry?
popculture.fact: The controversy led to multiple countries drafting emergency legislation regulating AI companion chatbots for minors.

popculture.question: The 'Woman Yelling at a Cat' meme combines a scene from 'Real Housewives of Beverly Hills' with a photo of a cat named what?
popculture.fact: Smudge the cat's owner said he always sits at the table like that — the meme photo was completely candid.

geography.question: Which African country contains the Danakil Depression, one of the hottest inhabited places on Earth with average temperatures exceeding 34°C year-round?
geography.fact: The Danakil Depression sits 125 meters below sea level and has lava lakes and neon-colored acid pools.

geography.question: True or False: Lake Chad, once one of Africa's largest lakes, has shrunk by approximately 90% since the 1960s.
options: It has actually grown since the 1960s
geography.fact: Lake Chad shrank from 25,000 km² to roughly 1,350 km², affecting 30 million people across four countries.

geography.question: In 2025, which African nation officially opened the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam's final turbines, completing the controversial megaproject?
geography.fact: The GERD is Africa's largest hydroelectric dam, generating 5,150 MW from the Blue Nile.

geography.question: The Richat Structure, a massive 40-km-wide geological formation sometimes called the 'Eye of the Sahara,' is located in which country?
geography.fact: Once thought to be a meteor impact, the Richat Structure is actually caused by geological erosion of a dome.

geography.question: Which desert receives so little rain that parts of it have had no recorded precipitation in over 500 years?
geography.fact: Despite extreme aridity, the Atacama hosts microbial life that NASA studies as a Mars analog.

geography.question: What is the name of the cold ocean current responsible for the extreme aridity of the Namib Desert along Africa's southwest coast?
geography.fact: The cold Benguela Current cools coastal air, preventing moisture from reaching land and creating coastal fog deserts.

geography.question: How many countries does the Karakum Desert, one of the world's hottest sand deserts, primarily span?
geography.fact: The Karakum covers about 70% of Turkmenistan and is home to the 'Door to Hell' gas crater.

geography.question: Which Asian country contains the world's largest cave passage by volume, Hang Sơn Đoòng?
geography.fact: Hang Sơn Đoòng is so vast it has its own localized weather system, including clouds and fog.

geography.question: The Sundarbans, the world's largest mangrove forest, is shared between India and which other country?
geography.fact: Sundarbans honey collectors wear face masks on the back of their heads to deter tiger attacks.

geography.question: Which Asian lake, located between Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan, was once the world's fourth-largest lake but has largely disappeared since the 1960s?
geography.fact: Rusted ships now sit stranded in desert where the Aral Sea's waters once stretched for miles.

sports.question: Which tennis Grand Slam tournament uniquely used a final-set super tiebreak (to 10 points) before any other Slam adopted one?
sports.fact: The Australian Open introduced the first-to-10 final-set tiebreak in 2019, pioneering the format among Slams.

sports.question: Who was the last player to win the French Open men's singles title on their tournament debut?
sports.fact: Mats Wilander won Roland Garros in 1982 as an unseeded 17-year-old on his very first attempt.

sports.question: True or False: No tennis player has ever won all four Grand Slam singles titles on the same surface type.
options: True — all Slams have always had different surfaces
False — all four were once played on grass
False — all four were once played on hard courts
False — all four were once played on clay
sports.fact: Until 1975, both the Australian and US Opens were played on grass, matching Wimbledon's surface.

sports.question: At the 2025 Australian Open, which player ended Novak Djokovic's tournament run in the semifinal?
sports.fact: Jannik Sinner defeated Djokovic and went on to successfully defend his Australian Open title in 2025.

sports.question: How many NBA teams has LeBron James played for during his career through the 2025-26 season?
sports.fact: LeBron played for Cleveland (twice), Miami, and the Lakers — technically three different franchises.

sports.question: Which NBA player holds the record for the most turnovers in a single game with 14?
sports.fact: Jason Kidd committed 14 turnovers in a 2000 game — the most by any player in NBA history.

sports.question: Which NBA arena sits at the highest elevation above sea level?
sports.fact: Salt Lake City's Delta Center sits at ~4,300 feet, slightly higher than Denver's Ball Arena at ~5,280 feet... actually Denver is higher. Correction: Ball Arena in Denver at 5,280 ft is highest.

sports.question: Who became the youngest person to run a sub-4-minute mile, achieving the feat at age 16 in 2025?
sports.fact: Tyrone DeLaGarza broke the age barrier in 2025, shattering assumptions about what teenage runners could achieve.

sports.question: Which sport saw its first officially recognized 'perfect game' only in the 21st century?
sports.fact: The first televised 9-dart finish happened in 1984, but it wasn't until the 2000s that they became regularly recognized feats.

sports.question: What surprising first did Diana Nyad accomplish in 2013, at the age of 64?
options: Swam the English Channel both ways nonstop
Swam from Cuba to Florida without a shark cage
Ran a marathon on every continent
sports.fact: Nyad swam 110 miles through shark and jellyfish-infested waters on her fifth attempt over 35 years.

food.question: Which Italian pasta shape's name literally translates to 'little worms'?
food.fact: Vermicelli comes from 'verme' (worm) — yet it's beloved worldwide despite its squirmy name.

food.question: In traditional Italian cuisine, what ingredient is strictly forbidden in a genuine carbonara sauce?
food.fact: Adding cream to carbonara is considered culinary heresy in Rome — the silkiness comes only from eggs and cheese.

food.question: In 2025, which Italian region was awarded a new UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage recognition for its traditional espresso coffee culture?
food.fact: Neapolitan espresso culture was recognized by UNESCO, honoring centuries of ritual coffee preparation.

food.question: True or False: The Italian dish 'cacio e pepe' traditionally uses more than five ingredients.
options: False — it uses only three: pasta, pecorino, and black pepper
False — it uses only two: pasta and cheese
True — it also requires butter and garlic
food.fact: Cacio e pepe's genius lies in its radical simplicity — just three ingredients create a complex, creamy sauce.

food.question: What percentage of the world's cocoa beans come from West Africa?
food.fact: Côte d'Ivoire and Ghana alone produce more cocoa than the rest of the world combined.

food.question: Which type of chocolate contains a naturally occurring compound called theobromine that is toxic to dogs but acts as a mild stimulant in humans?
options: Dark chocolate — it has the highest concentration
food.fact: Theobromine literally means 'food of the gods' — from Theobroma cacao — yet it can be lethal to pets.

food.question: What is 'bloom' on a chocolate bar?
options: A white-grey coating from fat or sugar crystals rising to the surface
A fermentation stage during cocoa processing
food.fact: Bloomed chocolate looks unappetizing but is perfectly safe to eat — it's just misaligned cocoa butter crystals.

food.question: Which traditional British pudding is steamed, made with suet, and studded with dried fruits, yet contains no plums?
food.fact: The word 'plum' was 17th-century slang for raisins — actual plums were never part of the recipe.

food.question: How many Scotch eggs must a 'substantial meal' contain, according to the famous 2020 UK pub debate during COVID-19 tier restrictions?
options: One — and it sparked a national argument
There was never an official number given
food.fact: The UK government never defined 'substantial meal,' turning the humble Scotch egg into a constitutional crisis.

food.question: Which unusual ingredient was traditionally used to give Stargazy pie from Cornwall its distinctive appearance?
options: Whole pilchard heads poking through the pastry crust
Seaweed wrapped around the pie edges
Crushed oyster shells baked into the lid
food.fact: Fish heads protrude through the crust so their oils drain back into the pie — waste nothing at sea.

music.question: Which famous opera features a character who literally freezes to death in a Parisian garret, with her final aria 'Sono andati?' sung while dying?
music.fact: Mimì's death scene in La Bohème is so quiet that the other characters don't realize she's died.

music.question: In 2025, which countertenor made headlines by becoming the first to sing the lead role in a new production of Handel's 'Giulio Cesare' at the Metropolitan Opera?
music.fact: Costanzo is also a contemporary art curator, blurring boundaries between opera and visual art.

music.question: True or False: Mozart's opera 'The Marriage of Figaro' was temporarily banned in Vienna before its premiere because the source play was considered politically subversive.
options: True — the Beaumarchais play was banned, not the opera itself
False — it was never restricted in any form
True — both the play and opera were fully banned
False — it was banned in Paris, not Vienna
music.fact: Emperor Joseph II banned the Beaumarchais play but allowed Mozart's opera version with a toned-down libretto.

music.question: Which Russian composer's Symphony No. 7, nicknamed 'Leningrad,' was performed in the besieged city in 1942 with starving musicians and broadcast through loudspeakers toward enemy lines?
music.fact: Some musicians were recalled from the front lines specifically to have enough players for the performance.

music.question: Which Beatles song was the first to feature only one Beatle performing, with Paul McCartney playing every instrument and singing all vocals?
options: The Ballad of John and Yoko
music.fact: Yesterday uses only McCartney's voice and acoustic guitar plus a string quartet — no other Beatles appear.

music.question: How many songs did The Beatles record for their legendary rooftop concert on January 30, 1969, before police shut it down?
music.fact: They played nine takes of five songs, including three versions of 'Get Back,' in about 42 minutes.

music.question: Which Beatles album originally included a sleeve with the band dressed in butcher smocks holding dismembered dolls, quickly recalled and replaced?
music.fact: Original 'butcher cover' copies of Yesterday and Today now sell for over $100,000 at auction.

music.question: Which 80s pop icon's 'Thriller' music video was directed by John Landis and cost approximately $500,000, making it the most expensive music video at the time?
music.fact: The 14-minute Thriller video was the first music video inducted into the National Film Registry.

music.question: What was Cyndi Lauper's original career before her solo pop breakthrough, which saw her working at an unexpected job to pay bills?
options: IHOP waitress and Japanese restaurant singer
music.fact: Lauper sang in a Japanese restaurant and waitressed at IHOP while recovering from vocal cord damage.

music.question: Which 80s synth-pop duo's debut album 'Welcome to the Pleasuredome' entered the UK chart at number one — a feat not achieved by a debut since the 1960s?
music.fact: Their single 'Relax' spent 48 consecutive weeks on the UK chart, partly boosted by a BBC ban.

technology.question: Which 1993 video game was secretly developed under the codename 'Project Doctor' to avoid attracting attention from competitors?
technology.fact: id Software used internal codenames to keep Doom's revolutionary 3D engine secret during development.

technology.question: True or False: The original Pac-Man arcade game has a kill screen that makes level 256 unbeatable due to an integer overflow bug.
technology.fact: The game uses an 8-bit integer for levels, causing half the screen to become garbled at level 256.

technology.question: In 2025, Nintendo's museum (Nintendo Museum) opened in which Japanese city, featuring playable exhibits spanning over a century of company history?
technology.fact: Nintendo was founded in Kyoto in 1889 as a playing card company — 96 years before Super Mario Bros.

technology.question: Which early home console was the first to use optical discs instead of cartridges, predating the PlayStation by several years?
technology.fact: The TurboGrafx-CD launched in 1988 in Japan, making CD-based gaming mainstream six years before PlayStation.

technology.question: What was the name of the first commercially available humanoid robot for home use, sold by Honda in limited quantities?
technology.fact: ASIMO was never sold commercially — Pepper by SoftBank was actually the first mass-market humanoid for homes.

technology.question: In 2025, Figure AI's humanoid robot made headlines by performing which surprising task autonomously in a BMW factory?
options: Inserting sheet metal parts into fixtures
technology.fact: Figure 02 robots began working alongside humans at BMW's Spartanburg plant handling precise physical tasks.

technology.question: How many axes of movement does a standard industrial robot arm typically have?
technology.fact: Six degrees of freedom match the human arm's versatility — three for position and three for orientation.

technology.question: Which programming language was originally named 'Oak' after a tree outside its creator's office window?
technology.fact: James Gosling renamed Oak to Java after a trademark conflict — inspired by Java coffee the team drank.

technology.question: Which programming language, created by Apple, shares its name with a famous satirist and was designed to replace Objective-C?
technology.fact: Swift was named after the bird, not Jonathan Swift — but Apple enjoys the literary double meaning.

technology.question: What unusual feature makes the esoteric programming language 'Whitespace' particularly counterintuitive to read?
options: All code must be written backwards
Only spaces, tabs, and newlines are meaningful
technology.fact: Whitespace ignores all visible characters — the actual program is hidden in the invisible formatting characters.

nature.question: Which bird of prey has been documented using fire — deliberately spreading wildfires by carrying burning sticks to flush out prey?
nature.fact: Australian Black Kites intentionally spread bushfires, making them one of the few animals to use fire as a tool.

nature.question: True or False: The Philippine Eagle, one of the world's largest raptors, primarily hunts monkeys in the wild.
nature.fact: The Philippine Eagle was formerly called the 'Monkey-eating Eagle' and flying lemurs are also a major prey item.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers tracking Eleonora's Falcons discovered they do something unusual to captured prey birds. What is it?
options: They pluck and store them alive in rock crevices
They drown them in seawater before eating
They feed them to their chicks still alive and flying
They trade prey with neighboring falcon pairs
nature.fact: Eleonora's Falcons imprison live birds by removing flight feathers and wedging them into rocks as living larders.

nature.question: Which raptor has the largest eyes relative to its body size, giving it exceptional low-light vision for nocturnal hunting?
nature.fact: Great Horned Owl eyes are nearly as large as human eyes despite the bird weighing only about 3 pounds.

nature.question: Which dog breed was originally developed by Korean royalty and has a distinctive blue-black tongue like the Chow Chow?
nature.fact: Shar Peis share the rare blue-black tongue trait with Chow Chows, suggesting an ancient genetic connection between the breeds.

nature.question: How many distinct vocalizations can a typical domestic cat produce, according to recent bioacoustic studies?
nature.fact: Cats have over 100 distinct vocalizations — dogs have only about 10 — and most are used exclusively with humans.

nature.question: What surprising ability do goats share with humans but not with most other domesticated animals?
options: They can recognize human facial expressions
They can sweat through their skin
nature.fact: Goats can distinguish between happy and angry human faces and prefer to interact with smiling people.

nature.question: The largest living organism on Earth is a honey fungus (Armillaria ostoyae) in Oregon. Approximately how large is it?
nature.fact: This single fungal organism spans 2,385 acres — roughly 1,665 football fields — and is estimated to be 8,650 years old.

nature.question: Which microorganism holds the record for surviving the longest in space, enduring 3 years on the exterior of the International Space Station?
nature.fact: Deinococcus radiodurans can withstand radiation 1,500 times the lethal human dose, earning it the nickname 'Conan the Bacterium.'

nature.question: True or False: Some fungi can generate their own electricity and communicate using electrical impulses in patterns resembling human language.
options: False — they communicate only through chemical signals
nature.fact: Researchers found fungi send electrical spikes through hyphae in clusters of up to 50 distinct signal patterns resembling words.

film.question: Which 2025 Cannes Palme d'Or-winning film was notably shot entirely on 16mm film in a single continuous location?
film.fact: Payal Kapadia's luminous Mumbai-set film made history as the first Indian film to win the Grand Prix at Cannes.

film.question: In the indie classic 'Eraserhead' (1977), what did David Lynch use to create the disturbingly lifelike baby puppet?
options: He has never publicly revealed it
film.fact: Lynch consistently refused to explain the baby's construction, calling it his most closely guarded secret.

film.question: Which arthouse director's debut feature was funded entirely by maxing out personal credit cards totaling $7,000?
film.fact: Shane Carruth's 'Primer' (2004) won the Sundance Grand Jury Prize despite its micro-budget origins.

film.question: True or False: The 2014 film 'Boyhood' by Richard Linklater was filmed over 12 consecutive years with the same cast.
options: False, they recast the lead actor midway
film.fact: Filming began in 2002 and ended in 2013, making it one of cinema's most ambitious longitudinal projects.

film.question: What groundbreaking technique did the 1927 silent film 'Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans' pioneer for moving camera shots?
options: Dolly tracks on a suspended rail system
Camera mounted on a moving trolley through forced-perspective sets
First use of a handheld Steadicam prototype
film.fact: Director F.W. Murnau built elaborate tracks through interconnected sets to create seamless, dreamlike camera movements.

film.question: Which silent film comedian performed a stunt where a real house facade fell around him, missing him by inches?
film.fact: In 'Steamboat Bill, Jr.' (1928), the 2-ton wall's open window cleared Keaton with just 2 inches of clearance.

film.question: Who was the first actor to win the Academy Award for Best Actor, at the very first Oscars ceremony in 1929?
film.fact: Jannings received his Oscar by mail before the ceremony and soon returned to Germany, later making Nazi propaganda films.

film.question: In 'The Texas Chain Saw Massacre' (1974), what was the actual temperature inside the house during the dinner scene filming?
film.fact: The sweltering Texas heat and decomposing real animal bones made actors genuinely nauseous during filming.

film.question: Which horror film used a reversed recording of a prayer as part of its iconic soundtrack?
film.fact: Jerry Goldsmith's 'Ave Satani' earned an Oscar nomination—it was the first satanic chant composed for a major film.

film.question: How many people actually die on-screen in John Carpenter's original 'Halloween' (1978)?
film.fact: Despite its terrifying reputation, Halloween shows remarkably little blood and relies almost entirely on suspense.

politics.question: Which current world leader previously worked as a bus driver before entering politics?
politics.fact: Maduro drove buses in Caracas for years and rose through union organizing to the presidency.

politics.question: As of early 2026, who serves as the Prime Minister of India?
politics.fact: Modi has served continuously since 2014, making him India's longest-serving non-Congress PM.

politics.question: Which world leader became the youngest sitting head of state in 2025 after taking office at age 34?
politics.fact: Boric took office at 36, but Chile has a tradition of electing younger leaders in Latin America.

politics.question: True or False: The current King of the United Kingdom, Charles III, is the oldest person ever to accede to the British throne.
politics.fact: Charles became king at age 73 in 2022, surpassing William IV who acceded at 64.

politics.question: In 2025, which country's prime minister resigned after a corruption scandal involving undisclosed payments from a foreign energy company?
politics.fact: Austrian politics has seen multiple leaders resign over corruption scandals in recent years.

politics.question: The 'Partygate' scandal, involving lockdown-violating gatherings at 10 Downing Street, ultimately contributed to whose resignation?
politics.fact: Police issued over 120 fixed penalty notices for parties held during strict COVID lockdowns.

politics.question: Which former South Korean president was impeached in 2024 after declaring martial law for only a few hours?
politics.fact: Yoon's martial law declaration lasted roughly six hours before the National Assembly voted to lift it.

politics.question: How many countries worldwide still do not allow women to vote in any national elections as of 2026?
politics.fact: Vatican City restricts voting to cardinals, but it is technically a theocracy, not a standard nation-state.

politics.question: Which country uses a mandatory voting system where citizens can be fined for failing to vote?
politics.fact: Australia has enforced compulsory voting since 1924, with typical turnout exceeding 90 percent.

politics.question: In which surprising year did New Zealand become the first self-governing country to grant all women the right to vote?
politics.fact: New Zealand's 1893 decision preceded most of Europe by decades—France didn't follow until 1944.
